column2: What are the symptoms of diabetes?
column4: Symptoms of diabetes include feeling very thirsty needing to urinate more often than usual blurred vision feeling tired and losing weight unintentionally. In Type 2 diabetes symptoms may be mild and take years to be noticed.

column2: How can Type 2 diabetes be prevented?
column4: Type 2 diabetes can be prevented by maintaining a healthy body weight staying physically active with at least 150 minutes of moderate exercise per week eating a healthy diet avoiding sugar and saturated fat and not smoking tobacco.

column2: What is the difference between Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes?
column4: Type 1 diabetes involves deficient insulin production and requires daily insulin injections. Type 2 diabetes is when the body cannot effectively use insulin — it is often preventable and related to lifestyle factors like weight and physical inactivity.

column2: What are the symptoms of hypertension?
column4: Most people with hypertension have no symptoms. Severely elevated blood pressure (over 180/120 mmHg) can cause severe headaches chest pain dizziness difficulty breathing nausea vomiting blurred vision confusion and nosebleeds.

column2: How is hypertension treated?
column4: Treatment includes lifestyle changes (low-salt diet weight loss physical activity quitting tobacco) and medications such as ACE inhibitors angiotensin-2 receptor blockers calcium channel blockers and diuretics.

column2: How is tuberculosis transmitted?
column4: TB is caused by bacteria that spread through the air when infected people cough sneeze or spit. It primarily affects the lungs.

column2: What are the symptoms of tuberculosis?
column4: TB symptoms include prolonged cough (sometimes with blood) chest pain weakness fatigue weight loss fever and night sweats. Symptoms can be mild for months allowing spread to others unknowingly.

column2: What is the treatment for tuberculosis?
column4: TB is treated with antibiotics including rifampicin isoniazid pyrazinamide and ethambutol taken daily for 4–6 months. Stopping early risks antibiotic resistance.

column2: What are the symptoms of dengue fever?
column4: Dengue symptoms include high fever (40°C/104°F) severe headache pain behind the eyes muscle and joint pains nausea vomiting swollen glands and rash. Symptoms appear 4–10 days after infection and last 2–7 days.

column2: What is severe dengue and what are its warning signs?
column4: Severe dengue is a rare but potentially fatal complication. Warning signs after fever subsides include severe abdominal pain persistent vomiting rapid breathing bleeding gums or nose fatigue restlessness and blood in vomit or stool.

column2: How is malaria prevented?
column4: Malaria prevention includes using insecticide-treated mosquito nets applying insect repellents containing DEET or Icaridin wearing protective clothing and indoor residual spraying. Chemoprophylaxis is recommended for travelers to endemic areas.

column2: What are the symptoms of anaemia?
column4: Common symptoms include fatigue and tiredness dizziness or lightheadedness cold hands and feet headaches and shortness of breath. Severe anaemia can cause pale skin and fingernails rapid breathing elevated heart rate.

column2: What causes anaemia?
column4: The most common cause is iron deficiency from inadequate diet. Other causes include deficiencies in folate vitamin B12 and vitamin A blood loss from parasitic infections or menstruation infections like malaria and inherited conditions like sickle cell disease and thalassemia.

column2: What are the symptoms of COVID-19?
column4: Most common symptoms are fever chills and sore throat. Less common symptoms include fatigue muscle aches runny nose headache cough and taste or smell changes. Severe symptoms requiring immediate care include difficulty breathing persistent chest pain and confusion.

column2: What are the common types of mental disorders?
column4: According to WHO anxiety disorders (affecting 359 million people) and depression (280 million) are the most prevalent. Others include bipolar disorder PTSD schizophrenia eating disorders and neurodevelopmental disorders like ADHD and autism spectrum disorder.

column2: Is the health information in these fact sheets applicable specifically to India?
column4: The indexed content is primarily WHO fact sheets (global scope) supplemented by MoHFW guidance (India-specific). The answer should distinguish global vs India-specific guidance per the system prompt.

column2: How is malaria different from dengue in terms of transmission?
column4: Malaria is transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes via parasite; dengue is transmitted by Aedes aegypti mosquitoes via virus. Both are mosquito-borne but different vectors and pathogens.
